game_name: Super Star Wars: Return of the Jedi

danger_signals.visual_form: Imperial stormtroopers, creatures, bosses with distinct sprite patterns
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring combat or evasion

danger_signals.visual_form: Large-scale enemy sprites (Jabba the Hutt, Rancor, Darth Vader, The Emperor)
danger_signals.meaning: Significant narrative and mechanical threat; stage climax

danger_signals.visual_form: Level design elements that block or damage player
danger_signals.meaning: Physical world danger independent of faction opposition
Accumulating damage; proximity to defeat state

safety_signals.meaning: Health restoration, weapon upgrades, temporary invulnerability

safety_signals.visual_form: Multiple playable character options per level
safety_signals.meaning: Gameplay flexibility and tactical choice for stage hazards

safety_signals.visual_form: Mode 7 pseudo-3D speeder bike and Millennium Falcon sections
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary tactical shift; different threat evaluation

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Character costumes: Luke Skywalker robes, Han Solo vest, Princess Leia outfit variants, Wicket Ewok appearance
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Characters appear in contextually appropriate costumes (Leia as Boushh bounty hunter, escaped slave, Rebel fighter)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Endor forest terrain, Millennium Falcon interior, Rebel base locations

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Stormtrooper uniform sprite pattern, Imperial insignia through level design
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Standard stormtrooper enemies vs. Darth Vader (unique large sprite) vs. The Emperor (final boss)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Death Star interior geometry, Imperial ship corridors, Jabba's Palace (Imperial-aligned)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Jabba's Palace visual theme, sail barge structure
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Jabba boss sprite, Rancor beast, palace guards and creatures
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tatooine desert, Jabba's palace interior, sail barge deck

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Tatooine environments (Jabba's Palace levels), characters in disguise/infiltration costumes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Leia appears in Boushh bounty hunter costume, emphasizing covert approach

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Death Star interior levels, increased stormtrooper enemy density, corridor-based geometry
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Luke forced to penetrate Imperial installations; combat-forward level design

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Forest terrain, speeder bike chase sequence in Mode 7 3D perspective
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Vehicular combat section; tempo shift from ground-based platforming

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Imperial throne room architecture, Vader and Emperor boss sprites
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Climactic boss sequence; narrative convergence of Rebel vs. Empire conflict

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Selectable characters with distinct sprite sets and visual appearances
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Princess Leia's appearance changes per story phase (Boushh → slave → Rebel leader); mechanical playstyle variations per character

environmental_storytelling.example: Princess Leia appears as Boushh (bounty hunter disguise) at Jabba's Palace, changes to broken-chain slave after escape, then Rebel uniform on Endor—visual narrative of her agency and role shift
Tatooine desert → Jabba's Palace → Death Star → Endor forest → Throne room sequence follows film narrative; environment communicates story progression
Jabba the Hutt, Rancor, Darth Vader, and The Emperor are visually distinct, large-scale sprite graphics—signal that story-critical conflicts are mechanically distinct from minion encounters
Mode 7 pseudo-3D speeder bike chase on Endor uses technical palette different from side-scrolling sections—signals narrative moment of open-world combat vs. linear infiltration
Stormtrooper sprite pattern repeats across Death Star and Imperial environments; visual cohesion communicates unified Imperial presence and threat structure

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Five Force powers available (reduced from predecessor's nine); easier recharge mechanic; cannot use blaster variation—mechanical differentiation from other characters
Wields staff weapon; infiltration-oriented playstyle in Jabba's Palace
Fights with broken chain as weapon; constrained mobility reflecting narrative captivity
Equipped with blaster; full combat capability; reflects command status and freedom
Melee-focused combat; distinct sprite from human characters
Small stature sprite; Endor-specific playability; represents indigenous Ewok alliance
Scripted patrol and attack patterns; ranged blaster fire; represent faceless Imperial opposition
Large boss sprite; unique attack pattern; mechanical complexity exceeds standard enemies
Ultimate boss sprite; climactic encounter signaling narrative resolution

notes: Super Star Wars: Return of the Jedi uses classic 16-bit platformer visual language adapted to Star Wars IP. Manifestation layer relies on: (1) Character visual identity tied to narrative role (Leia's costume changes encode story progression); (2) Faction cohesion through sprite consistency (Stormtroopers as uniform visual threat); (3) Environmental theming (Tatooine → Death Star → Endor) as world-state indicator; (4) Boss scale and uniqueness as encounter significance signal; (5) Vehicle sequences as mechanical state-shift visual marker. Limited sources provide minimal detail on exact visual mechanics—extraction based on gameplay descriptions and character role documentation. Game follows film narrative closely, so visual communication is primarily diegetic (story-driven) rather than abstract HUD-based.
